ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for curry puff specifically; mapped to the nearest parent class 'pastry food product' (FOODON_03302050) as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for curry puff; A0FKP (filled pastry, deep-fried) is the closest defensible category anchor in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for curry puff as a specific food item; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard curry puff weighs approximately 80 g and constitutes a typical single-serve snack portion as commonly sold at hawker centres and bakeries in Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — Snacks and Pastries (Curry Puff / Karipap)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for curry puff (karipap) with chicken and potato filling, deep-fried. Saturated fat estimated at 35% of total fat based on typical palm oil / shortening frying medium consistent with HPB Singapore dietary fat guidance. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and water content are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore source for this item and are set to -1. Cholesterol estimated at 55 mg per 100 g based on egg and chicken content consistent with USDA FoodData Central analogous filled pastry entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese-style spiral curry puff. GI of 65 is estimated by analogy to deep-fried filled wheat-flour pastry (similar to samosa / fried pastry puff) reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The high fat content (~22.5 g/100 g) and protein/fibre from the filling moderate the glycaemic response of the refined flour crust and potato filling. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 26.24) / 100 ≈ 17.1 g, using 80 g serving size (32.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 0.80 = 26.24 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg/100 g) and elevated saturated fat from deep-frying in palm-based oil
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit curry puff intake to no more than one piece (80 g) per occasion, as a single serving provides approximately 304 mg sodium (~13% of the 2,300 mg daily limit) and significant saturated fat that can contribute to arterial stiffness and elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycaemic index (GI 65) with moderate carbohydrate load (GL ~17 per serving) from refined wheat flour crust and potato filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat curry puffs as an occasional snack only, limiting to one piece per sitting and pairing with a low-GI food or fibre source to blunt the postprandial glucose response; the medium GI and moderate glycaemic load require careful portion control within the daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (22.5 g/100 g) with substantial saturated fat (~7.9 g/100 g) from deep-frying, combined with elevated sodium and energy density (360 kcal/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of curry puffs due to the high saturated fat and sodium content that can raise LDL cholesterol and blood pressure; if consumed, limit to one piece infrequently and choose lower-fat cooking alternatives where available.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried refined-grain product with pro-inflammatory potential from oxidised frying oils and refined carbohydrates
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit deep-fried snacks such as curry puffs, as repeated consumption of foods high in refined grains and oxidised fats may exacerbate systemic inflammation and contribute to flare frequency.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Curry Puffs (Karipap / 咖喱角) are a quintessential deep-fried savoury snack of Chinese and Southeast Asian origin, widely popular in Singapore, Malaysia, and across the broader region. The defining characteristic of the Chinese-style variety is its 'thousand-layer' or spiral flaky pastry shell, achieved by laminating a water-based outer dough with a fat-based inner dough through repeated folding and rolling — a technique that produces a shattering, multi-layered crust upon deep-frying. The filling is a fragrant, hearty mixture of diced potatoes, minced or diced chicken, sliced onions, and hard-boiled egg, seasoned generously with curry powder, turmeric, and aromatic spices. The puffs are deep-fried in palm-based or vegetable oil until golden brown, yielding a crisp exterior that contrasts with the soft, spiced interior. Nutritionally, each 80 g puff provides approximately 288 kcal, 6.8 g protein, 18 g fat, and 26.2 g carbohydrate, with 304 mg sodium. The medium glycaemic index (~65) is moderated by the fat and protein content of the filling. Clinically, curry puffs are energy-dense and high in saturated fat and sodium, making them a food to limit for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or Type 2 Diabetes. They are best enjoyed as an occasional breakfast or afternoon tea snack rather than a daily staple. Culturally, curry puffs are a beloved hawker-centre and bakery staple, commonly eaten at breakfast, as a mid-morning snack, or during afternoon tea, and are a fixture at festive gatherings and school canteens across Singapore and Malaysia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a curry puff?
Is curry puff suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium in a curry puff?
What is the GI of curry puff?
Spiral curry puff vs baked curry puff
Can I eat curry puff if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore food composition data for curry puff (karipap) with chicken and potato filling, which is the closest approved primary source for this Singaporean hawker snack. Saturated fat is estimated from typical frying medium composition rather than directly measured. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar deep-fried filled wheat-flour pastry, as no direct GI measurement for Chinese-style spiral curry puff is published in approved sources. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat) and moisture data for deep-fried curry puff (karipap) are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore food composition database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2024. These four fields cannot be populated from any approved source without fabrication and are therefore set to the sentinel value -1 per the Veritas numeric hygiene rule. Saturated_fat_g (7.9 g/100 g) is estimated at approximately 35% of total fat based on typical palm olein / vegetable shortening frying medium composition, consistent with HPB Singapore dietary fat guidance for deep-fried snacks. Cholesterol_mg (55 mg/100 g) is estimated from egg and chicken content by analogy with USDA FoodData Central entries for similar filled pastry products. The duplicate portion entry in the input JSON (two identical 80 g entries) has been deduplicated to a single 80 g default serving. All core macros (protein_g 8.5, fat_g 22.5, carbohydrate_g 32.8) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a deep-fried filled pastry product; no zero-macro justification is required. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for Chinese-style spiral curry puff should be sought from the University of Sydney GI Database in a future update.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Curry Puffs', a precise canonical English name. All mandatory Singaporean-cuisine local-language names are present: Chinese (咖喱角), Malay (Karipap), Tamil (கறி பஃப்), Hindi (करी पफ). G6 satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (360.0), protein_g (8.5), fat_g (22.5), carbohydrate_g (32.8) all present, numeric, and non-zero. Sourced from HPB Singapore food composition data (adapted) as documented in nutrient_source.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (2.1), sodium_mg (380.0), saturated_fat_g (7.9), sugar_g (1.8) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible for a deep-fried filled pastry.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (65), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (17.1), and gi_source all present. GI sourced by analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 with full documentation.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn approximate anchor (FOODON_03302050, 'pastry food product') and FoodEx2 approximate anchor (A0FKP, 'filled pastry / savoury pastry — deep-fried') provided with honest match-type ('closest_parent') and gap notes. SNOMED CT left null with documented gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,450 characters), covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms are populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three mandatory conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, human_condition_name correctly set), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid arthritis included as additional condition.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases array contains 11 entries covering English variants, Chinese script, Cantonese romanization, Mandarin romanization, Malay variants, Tamil, and Hindi. aliases_by_language is fully populated. All strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Self-check passed.

Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 validation feedback (iteration 1).

Expanded nutrient_source.source_match_notes to also reference the -1 fields and their unavailability basis (iteration 3).
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: EC000034 Curry Puffs passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The G3 numeric hygiene failure from iteration 2 has been resolved: all four -1 sentinel fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g) are now explicitly documented in the notes field with the reason for unavailability and the approved sources consulted that confirmed the data gap. All mandatory Singaporean-cuisine multilingual names (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are present. Core macros are complete and non-zero. Secondary nutrients are present. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database with full documentation. Ontology anchors are honest approximate matches with gap notes. Health context covers all three mandatory conditions with clinical specificity. LLM description exceeds 200 characters. Alias coverage is comprehensive and internally consistent.
